title: anchored: required+observers broadcast ring (third machine)
description: GRILL DECISIONS (2026-07-05): (1) Shape: REQUIRED consumers (registry, producer min-gates on them, lossless, full spmc API incl. pop_ref borrows — gating makes their reads tear-free) + unbounded lossy OBSERVERS (broadcast copy-out API, Lagged). 'At least one has read' = 1 required. (2) Module rust_rb::anchored; types Anchor (required) + Observer. (3) Full surface: element -> bytes -> shm (kinds 7/8; anchors = consumer table + leases; observers = PROT_READ lease-free). Same per-commit gates (Miri + adversarial audit). Composition: broadcast slot layout (seq words, word-atomic producer writes) + spmc gate machinery (registry, selective refresh, fetch_or-before-fence choreography) + broadcast write bracket & tail. T: NoUninit + Send + Sync. Lands in PR #8.

title: Comprehensive user documentation for rust-rb
description: The crate is feature-complete and hardened (6-round adversarial review, CI green). Public API has zero missing-docs, but existing docs are reference-style/terse; users need task-oriented, decision-guiding documentation to adopt and configure the queues correctly. Goal: go from 'I have a producer/consumer problem' to a correctly-configured, well-performing ring without reading the source. See HANDOFF.md for the full plan. Docs only — no API/behavior changes; any behavior gap found becomes its own issue.

title: Bytes-ring producer liveness + drain staleness (found by whole-repo max review)
description: Three confirmed bugs in the extracted cursor engine, all with verified repros: (1) pop of a padded frame from an exact-full byte ring skipped the immediate was-full flush (pad skip committed to read_cursor before the release accounting); (2) was_full only fired at occupancy == capacity while a byte producer blocks whenever contiguous space < pad+record, so pops deferred release to the batch boundary; (3) drain() bounded itself by the stale cached cursor when non-empty, missing freshly published messages. Fix: per-ring full watermark in cursor::advance (element ring: mask; byte ring: capacity/2, sound because records are capped at capacity/2), plus ConsumerCore::refresh() for drain. Regression tests: pop_releases_producer_blocked_on_padding, pop_of_padded_frame_from_exact_full_publishes, drain_sees_messages_published_after_cache_went_stale.

title: broadcast: caught-up spinning consumers couple the producer (k-independence gate FAILED)
description: Bench gate §5.7 (P-F9) failed on GB10/X925 (2026-07-05): lossy producer ns/push vs k caught-up PINNED SPINNING consumers: k=0 4.5, k=1 25.3, k=2 34.6, k=4 65.5 — pre-registered flat shape decisively violated (5.6x at k=1). Diagnostic: consumer ns/msg == producer ns/push to 2 decimals (lockstep); a LAGGED consumer decouples the producer to 8.7 ns/push, so the cost is the caught-up regime (tail line + slot lines pulled Shared per push). Note SPSC tight-spin (NoOp) is ~6 ns on this box, so the lossy gap is ~4x beyond the unavoidable one-polled-line ping-pong — candidates: (a) producer writes TWO shared lines per push (slot seq/payload + tail) vs SPSC's one; (b) bench consumers used raw try_pop tight loops (NoOp-equivalent) — re-measure with PauseWait/BackoffWait pop() which may back off the polling pressure; (c) X925 cluster-interconnect effects — re-measure on Grace before concluding the design misses §3.8. Mitigation ideas if real: amortized tail publication (every-j with latency tradeoff), consumer tail-read damping, hybrid slot-seq wait after first tail confirm. Design targets in docs/design/spmc.md §3.8; bench in examples/bench_broadcast.rs.
notes: ATTRIBUTION COMPLETE (GB10/X925, 2026-07-12, examples/probe_ring_scaling.rs + a temporary every-8th-push tail-publication hack): the k-coupling splits into (1) DOMINANT ~55-65%: the per-push tail store into k spinning readers — every push's tail line gets stolen immediately by the spin-loop, so the producer pays a coherence round per push; amortizing tail publication every 8 pushes cuts k=1 25.95->12.67, k=2 35.6->16.6, k=4 56.6->19.6, k=8 144.6->53.4 ns/push (even k=0 improves 4.5->3.54). (2) REMAINDER: the seqlock slot-line ping-pong per message (producer 3 stores, consumer 2 seq loads + copy) x k stealers. NOT adjacent-slot false sharing: line-isolating slots makes it WORSE (k=1 43.1 vs 25.95) — the 4-slots-per-line layout amortizes coherence transfers and should be kept. Mitigation is a real design tradeoff: batched tail publication breaks per-push visibility latency, and the SPSC-style ADAPTIVE publish is impossible here (producer keeps zero consumer knowledge by design) — a fixed batch knob or documented accept-and-use-Pause guidance are the honest options. Same-cluster baselines reproduced recorded numbers (k=1 25.95 vs 25.3; k=2 35.6 vs 34.6).

title: shm test gaps: at-least-once redelivery + seqlock rejection
description: Two real coverage gaps found in an shm code review (the two 'CRITICAL' findings in the same review were false positives — see the bd note). (1) At-least-once consumer recovery: crash_recovery_drains_everything_published (tests/shm.rs:109) proves no-loss, but nothing asserts the at-least-once REDELIVERY window when a consumer dies consumed-but-unpublished (deferred window capacity/8, or an in-flight Msg/drain batch). Add a test that kills a consumer mid-drain / holding a Msg and asserts the documented redelivery bound. (2) Seqlock/concurrent-create rejection: the generation-odd ('ring is being initialized') and 'ring was re-initialized during validation' paths (shm.rs:510,575) have no deterministic test. Add one that manually sets an odd generation / bumps generation between header read and lease claim and asserts attach fails.
close_reason: Added tests/shm.rs consumer_death_holding_msg_redelivers_at_least_once (at-least-once redelivery of an in-flight Msg after recovery) and shm.rs unit tests odd_generation_is_rejected_as_initializing + even_generation_attaches (seqlock generation guard). The true concurrent create/attach RACE is inherently non-deterministic; the deterministic guard paths are now covered.

title: Migration notes from cpp-fastchan
description: Short guide for users coming from geseq/cpp-fastchan: name mapping (put/get -> push/pop), SPSC-only scope (MPSC not ported), type-level single-P/C enforcement, runtime vs compile-time capacity, and the behavioral additions (adaptive publish, zero-copy, shm).
close_reason: Descoped at user request: the cpp-fastchan lineage isn't relevant to rust-rb usage. Guide removed (docs/guide/migration.md, guide::migration module, crate-hub link).

title: Doctested examples on every public method
description: Add a runnable rust example to each public method lacking one so docs are executable and CI-verified (cargo test runs doctests). Prioritize the non-obvious: claim/commit_init, pop_ref, drain, try_* full/empty behavior, shm constructors (no_run).

title: Shared-memory / IPC guide + runnable example
description: A complete two-process worked example (committed, e.g. examples/ipc_pair.rs or a doc walkthrough): memfd/shm_open, create_shm + drop-half + fd hand-off, attach in the peer, CrossProcess + ShmItem constraints, the trust model, and the lease/lifecycle + crash-recovery story (force_attach single-side, recover both, at-least-once bounds). Highest-value gap for the shm feature.
close_reason: Written and merged in the user-documentation pass: guide module + markdown guides + crate hub + ipc_pair example; validated (doc -D warnings, doctests, fmt, clippy) and adversarially fact-checked against source.

title: Semantics & gotchas doc
description: One authoritative 'behavior you must know' page: producer-side len/is_empty/is_full transiently over-count under backpressure (consumer-side exact); mem::forget on Msg/PopRef = re-delivery not leak; PopRef/drain panic-safety; the single-P/single-C contract and what breaks it; drop ordering; wrap-around safety. Cross-link from every affected method.

title: Performance tuning guide
description: Document: core pinning (core-to-core topology dominates; taskset), the adaptive read-cursor publish and its observable effects (batched under backpressure, per-element when caught up/starving), cache-line/false-sharing design, and a reproducible benchmarking methodology (examples, pinned, 2 runs). Measured numbers with machine caveats.

title: API usage guide (which method when)
description: Task guide over the whole method surface: push vs try_push; pop vs try_pop vs pop_ref (by-value drain-fast vs zero-copy read-in-place); claim/commit/commit_init; drain (batched). Byte ring: push/claim(len)->WriteSlot, pop->Msg, drain. Worked example per pattern; a use-case -> method table.

title: Crate-level guide (lib.rs)
description: Rewrite the lib.rs crate doc from quick-start into a proper guide: the mental model (monotonic masked cursors, split Producer/Consumer handles, single-P/single-C enforced by !Clone), a module map (spsc / spsc_bytes / wait / shm), the feature flags (shm + target gating), and a 'which ring do I want' pointer. Keep the runnable quick-start. Link out to the deeper guides.

title: Configuration guide (capacity + wait strategy)
description: rustdoc guide page + README section: (1) capacity — power-of-two rounding, the /8 (max 64 elem / 4096 byte) adaptive-publish window, memory footprint, burst vs steady sizing; (2) wait-strategy decision matrix — Pause/Yield/NoOp/CvWait latency/CPU/oversubscription/power trade-offs with concrete 'pick X when' guidance; (3) per-side P vs C strategy choice.

title: Shared-memory backing store for both ring buffers (IPC)
description:
Allow RingBuffer AND BytesRingBuffer to be backed by shared memory (e.g. memfd/shm_open + mmap) so producer and consumer can live in different processes.

Groundwork already in place: capacity is a runtime constructor argument (no compile-time size in the types), and all concurrency state is consolidated in the crate-private cursor engine (src/cursor.rs: Shared + ProducerCore/ConsumerCore) — the extraction PR (#4) means only ONE module needs to learn about alternative storage.

Design notes / open questions:
- Shared<B, P, C> currently lives in an Arc and owns Box<[B]>; an shm variant needs the whole shared state (cursor atomics + buffer) placed IN the mapped region, with the handles holding the mapping instead of an Arc. Suggest an internal storage abstraction (owned-heap vs mapped-region) under the existing cores so the hot path stays pointer+mask and does not change.
- Layout must be stable across processes: repr(C) header (cursor atomics, mask, magic/version/capacity fields for attach validation) followed by the buffer; the 128-byte CachePadded alignment must be preserved inside the mapping.
- Constructor/attach API: e.g. RingBuffer::create_shm(path_or_fd, capacity) -> Producer and RingBuffer::attach_shm(path_or_fd) -> Consumer (unsafe or validated attach — attaching to a hostile/corrupt region must not be UB: validate magic, capacity power-of-two, cursor sanity; document trust model).
- Wait strategies: spin strategies (NoOp/Pause/Yield) work cross-process as-is; CvWait does NOT (process-local mutex/condvar) — either restrict shm rings to spin strategies at the type level or add a futex-based cross-process strategy (FUTEX_WAIT on the cursor word) later.
- T for the fixed ring must be constrained for IPC (no pointers/non-POD): likely a `Pod`-style unsafe marker trait or restrict the shm variant to BytesRingBuffer first, since byte payloads are inherently process-safe; fixed-ring shm support can follow.
- Consumer Drop publish/notify semantics and the adaptive publish rule carry over unchanged (they only touch the in-mapping atomics).
- Lifetime/teardown: whoever unmaps last; crash-safety story (a dead producer mid-write_frame leaves an unpublished record — cursors stay consistent since publish is a single Release store; document).
- Verify no hot-path regression for the existing heap-backed constructors (A/B examples/bench and examples/bench_bytes, ~860-900 M msgs/s and ~4.9 ns/msg on pinned Grace cores).

comments.author: geseq
comments.text: Additional requirement (user): crash recovery. If the producer or consumer process dies, a surviving or restarting process must be able to recover everything in the shm store: attach to the existing region, validate header/cursors, and drain all fully-published records (the single Release-store publish means the region is always in a consistent state — an in-progress unpublished record is simply invisible and its space reclaimable once the producer role is re-attached). Recovery API sketch: RingBuffer::recover_shm(fd) -> (Producer, Consumer) or a drain-only attach; must handle stale cursor caches (rebuild handle caches from the shared atomics on attach) and define single-writer re-attachment safety (e.g. pid/lease field in the header to fence zombie writers).

title: Extract shared SPSC cursor/publish core used by both rings
description:
The max code review (PR #2) flagged that RingBuffer (src/spsc.rs) and BytesRingBuffer (src/spsc_bytes.rs) duplicate ~150 lines of concurrency-critical code near-verbatim: Inner layout (padded cursor atomics + wait strategies), the NonNull handle-caching constructor block, the reload-once has_space/has_item checks and wait loops, flush(), the adaptive publish rule (caught-up / was-full / batch-boundary), and the Consumer Drop publish+notify.

Drift already happened once within PR #2 itself: the bytes ring shipped without the publish-batch clamp that the fixed ring had (review finding, since fixed).

Task: extract a crate-private cursor-core module (shared Inner, handle-cached pointer set, occupancy checks on wrapped cursor differences, wait loops, adaptive flush machinery) that both rings instantiate — the fixed ring at element granularity, the bytes ring at byte granularity with its own batch clamp (publish_batch vs publish_batch_bytes) and frame layer on top.

Constraints:
- No hot-path regression: re-run examples/bench (expect ~860-900 M msgs/s pinned spin strategies on Grace cores 4/5) and examples/bench_bytes (~4.9 ns per 8B msg) before/after.
- Preserve all review-fix semantics: wrap-safe comparisons, was-full immediate flush, Drop publish+notify, PopRef drop-guard panic safety, drain at-most-once.
- Keep the public API unchanged (RingBuffer/BytesRingBuffer, push/pop naming, WaitStrategy bounds on handles).
close_reason: Implemented in PR #4 (src/cursor.rs shared engine); closed on main but stale in this branch's DB export

comments.author: geseq
comments.text: Implemented in PR #4 (https://github.com/geseq/rust-rb/pull/4): src/cursor.rs shared engine (Shared + ProducerCore/ConsumerCore + SlotCleanup teardown trait); rings are thin layers; public API unchanged; A/B benchmarks show zero hot-path regression (fixed 870-905 M msgs/s, bytes 4.8-5.0 ns/msg); byte ring gains was-full immediate flush. CI running.

title: bench: multi-consumer rings over shm (cross-process spmc + PROT_READ broadcast observers)
description: bench_shm.rs covers only the SPSC rings. Missing: cross-process gating (spmc create_shm + child producer, N consumer attaches with lease slots) and cross-process lossy (PROT_READ observer attach). Expected near-heap numbers same-process (same hot path); the cross-process delta is topology/TLB. Crib the fork pattern from examples/bench_shm.rs. Lower priority than the heap grids (now covered by bench_spmc_bytes/bench_broadcast_bytes/bench_anchored, 2026-07-12).

title: spmc: caught-up N-scaling not flat (producer +114% at N=4)
description: Bench §5.2 on GB10/X925 (2026-07-05): gating producer ns/op with N caught-up consumers (all keeping up, Yield): N=1 2.50, N=2 3.09 (+24%), N=4 5.35 (+114%). Within §2.9's saturated bound (<=2x SPSC at N=8 territory) but the pre-registered flat shape failed. Same family as the broadcast k-independence issue (lockstep caught-up coupling); investigate together, re-measure on Grace, and consider whether the per-consumer adaptive-publish caught-up regime (publish per element) multiplied by N polled cursor lines is the mechanism (P-F3's straggler fix addressed the backed-up regime; the caught-up regime may want deferral too when N>1). Numbers + methodology in examples/bench_spmc.rs.
notes: ROOT CAUSE FOUND (GB10/X925, 2026-07-12, examples/probe_ring_scaling.rs): adjacent-slot false sharing. 8 i64 slots share a cache line; N caught-up consumers copying slot s steal the line the producer writes at s+1..s+7. Evidence: same-cluster (15-19) producer ns/push with N=1/2/4 caught-up Yield consumers: shared-layout i64 3.66/6.95/12.65 vs line-isolated 64B element 3.89/3.90/4.11 — FLAT when slots are isolated (+6% at N=4 vs +246%). Synthetic floor (probe_coherence): a publish line with k spin-readers costs the writer <2ns even at k=8, so write_cursor fan-out is NOT the mechanism. Cross-cluster placement (X925 cluster A=5-9, B=15-19) inflates everything ~3-6x — N=8 on this box is necessarily mixed-cluster (41.5 shared / 13.9 padded). Mitigation options (user decision): document element-padding guidance (users pad T to 64B for flat N-scaling), or an opt-in padded-slot layout knob. Re-measure on Grace still advised for absolutes, but the mechanism is layout, not protocol.

title: spmc_bytes: tighten the starving lag filter with the producer's actual span
description: The M-F8 lag filter threshold capacity - max_record_span degenerates to ALIGN=4 bytes because the span is worst-case (2*record_len(max_message_len) - ALIGN = capacity - ALIGN): only fully-caught-up consumers get filtered; any consumer with >=1 unpublished record publishes per-message during a starvation episode (delta-audit finding, 2026-07-05). Refinement: the starving flag is an AtomicUsize — store the producer's ACTUAL required span (pad+record of the pending push) instead of 1; consumers filter on capacity - that_span, which is tight per episode. Needs the N-consumer backpressure bench (commit-7 suite) to prove it matters before changing protocol; verify no deadlock (filter must remain conservative under stale write_cache/published views).
close_reason: Implemented (review finding 12, salvage pass): the starving flag now stores the blocked push's actual required span (pad+record, exact per episode since a blocked producer's write cursor freezes frame()); consumers filter with capacity - span against published occupancy. Applied to spmc_bytes AND anchored_bytes for consistency; docs + filter tests updated; conservative-under-staleness argument documented in advance(). The N-consumer backpressure bench remains available to quantify the win, but the protocol change was mandated by the review finding.

title: no-feature cargo doc has broken intra-doc links to shm-gated items
description: cargo doc WITHOUT features + RUSTDOCFLAGS=-D warnings fails: src/lib.rs crate-hub links to shm-gated items (RingBuffer::create_shm, guide::shm_ipc) resolve only under --all-features. CI's docs job uses --all-features so CI is green; latent wart only. Fix: cfg_attr the hub bullets or use cfg-gated doc strings. Found during spmc_bytes Miri-race debugging (pre-existing on the branch).

title: shm doc polish: native-endian note, ShmItem example, AnchorKind docs, README ipc_pair link
description: Low-risk doc/comment improvements from an shm review. (1) Document that the shm wire format is NATIVE-endian and same-host-only (two local processes share endianness; arch_bits guards usize width) — NOT 'little-endian' as the review suggested. (2) Add a usage example to the ShmItem trait doc (shm.rs:114). (3) Add inline docs to the AnchorKind enum. (4) README shared-memory section: link the new runnable examples/ipc_pair.rs. Note: 'from_le vs from_le_bytes inconsistency' from the review is NOT real (spsc_bytes uses from_le consistently; from_le_bytes is only the shm magic string constant).
close_reason: Done: native-endian/same-host note added to shm module doc (+ fixed layout-table ordering), ShmItem usage example added, README links examples/ipc_pair.rs. AnchorKind was already fully documented (review item moot); 'from_le vs from_le_bytes' confirmed not a real inconsistency.

title: shm: drain-only recovery attach
description: Optional: a drain-only recovery/attach that maps an existing shm ring read-only-ish to drain everything published without claiming the producer role, complementing recover_shm (both roles). Sketched in the original cz2 design note; not a defect. Low priority.

title: SPMC broadcast ring (single-producer, multi-consumer)
description:
Extend the crate beyond SPSC with a single-producer / multi-consumer *broadcast* ring: one producer, N consumers, every consumer observes every message (fan-out / pub-sub, LMAX-Disruptor style). This is the natural fit for the existing monotonic-masked-cursor design; a work-distribution variant (each message to exactly one consumer) is a separate, harder design and out of scope here.

Design considerations:
- Gating: the producer may not overwrite a slot until ALL consumers have passed it. The single write-side gating cursor becomes a min-reduction over N per-consumer read cursors (each CachePadded to avoid false sharing; cost scales with N). Producer waits on the slowest consumer.
- Consumer set is fixed at construction (const-generic N or a Vec of handles) since gating must know the full set; late-joining consumers are a follow-up.
- No by-value pop: a broadcast slot is shared, so consumption is read-in-place only (pop_ref / Msg-style borrow). Payloads must outlive the slowest reader; T likely needs Copy/Clone (or the consumer only borrows). This breaks the current pop()->T drain-fast path.
- Cursor engine: today's ConsumerCore is single-consumer. SPMC needs a multi-consumer gating variant; likely a distinct SpmcBroadcast type rather than retrofitting Spsc, though the Shared header / adaptive-publish machinery can be reused.
- Adaptive publish rule carries over (touches only in-region atomics), but 'caught up' is now per-consumer.
- Scope first cut: fixed-element ring, broadcast semantics, spin wait strategies. Bytes-ring SPMC (variable frames + per-consumer positions) and shm/cross-process SPMC are follow-ups.
- Wait strategy: CvWait needs per-consumer notification; spin strategies work as-is.

Relates to the SPSC-only scope noted in rust-rb-1q3.8 (migration). Backlog / low priority; raise to its own epic if pursued.
notes:
REQUIREMENTS REVISED by user (2026-07-05): SPMC must support BOTH consumption semantics as options: (1) LOSSY broadcast — the slowest consumer LOSES messages, producer never blocks (overwrites; consumer detects overrun/lag); (2) GATING broadcast — the slowest consumer BLOCKS the producer (Disruptor-style min-gating, lossless). Study the LMAX Disruptor for performance ideas (multicast, gating-sequence caching, batch consumption) — performance is critical; nothing gets added 'for the sake of it'. Design must be thought through deeply and grilled with the user BEFORE any implementation; record decisions as ADRs.
GRILL ROUND 1 DECISIONS (user, 2026-07-05): (1) Membership: FULLY DYNAMIC subscribe/unsubscribe (user notes broadcast gating/lossy are NOT the only semantics — SHARDING/work-distribution semantics must be part of the design space analysis). (2) v1 scope: element AND bytes rings, heap AND shm — full surface. (3) Gating mode: any T: Send, producer drops-on-overwrite (heap; shm stays ShmItem/Copy). (4) Naming: rust_rb::spmc::RingBuffer (gating/lossless) + rust_rb::broadcast::RingBuffer (lossy). PROCESS: think deeply, research Disruptor/Aeron/prior art, ADVERSARIAL AUDITS of the design before implementation; record decisions as ADRs (grill-with-docs).
GRILL ROUND 2 DECISION (user, 2026-07-05) — ADR candidate 'lossy-read soundness policy': target BOTH x86-64 and ARM (no TSO-only shortcuts; real acquire fences, never signal-fence-only). Reader copy protocol starts with the STRICTER formally-sound variant (word/chunk-wise relaxed-atomic copy, validate-after via acquire fence + seq re-check) and gets BENCHMARKED first; any relaxation to a racier plain-copy variant must be justified by measured cost on both architectures (bench on Grace/aarch64 locally + x86_64 CI), behind an explicit choice — not assumed. Rationale: Boehm 2012 / P1478 — the racy copy is formally UB; TSO makes it benign on x86 only; we ship aarch64-first. Also keeps Miri/loom viable for the lossy engine.
GRILL ROUND 3 DECISION (user, 2026-07-05): proceed with the CURRENT approach — the two broadcast machines (spmc:: gating + broadcast:: lossy). The other SPMC semantics (competing consumers, keyed sharding, load balance) have inherently different perf profiles; they are DEFERRED — revisit per-semantics once the broadcast machines are built and benchmarked. Work-distribution research will be attached as background when it completes. Next: design doc -> adversarial audits -> final grill -> phased implementation.
DISTRIBUTION RESEARCH VERDICT (2026-07-05, full report in session): (A) competing-consumer ring = DO NOT BUILD — claim floor is 1 shared RMW/msg (~5-10x the 1.15ns SPSC baseline uncontended; DPDK measured 8->983 cyc/obj contended 1->32 threads); LMAX built WorkerPool (per-msg CAS retry loop, no batching possible, silent drop on handler exception), never optimized it, removed it in Disruptor 4.0 ('not a good fit for the underlying technology', issue #323/PR #324); moodycamel MPMC is internally per-producer sub-queues (concedes composition). (B) sharding/load-balance = thin router over N SPSC rings (Seastar smp_message_queue NxN SPSC pairs, DPDK RSS, FastFlow thesis, exchange sequencer->matcher); producer pays hash + uncontended SPSC push. (C) round-robin = broadcast + sequence%N==ordinal filter idiom (official Disruptor 4.x answer, Barker #277) — document, don't build. Novel bench opportunity flagged: '1 ring + modulo filter vs N rings + router vs Vyukov MPMC' comparison would be original README material. Single-writer principle numbers (Thompson): 1 thread 300ms vs 2 threads+CAS 18000ms.
FINAL GRILL OUTCOMES (2026-07-05) — DESIGN CLOSED: (1) heap membership UNBOUNDED via append-only 64-slot chunk registry (blocks never move); shm max_consumers fixed at create (physical). (2) Closed contract adopted: pop -> Result<T,Closed> (gating) / Result<T, PopError{Lagged{missed},Closed}> (lossy); SubscribeError{Closed,Full(shm-only)}; zero-consumer push succeeds; consumer detach notifies producer wait. (3) Lossy reposition: oldest+slack, slack CONFIGURABLE constructor knob default capacity/8; skip_to_latest(); pop naming kept. (4) Wait strategies v1: self-timed family NoOp/Pause/Yield + NEW BackoffWait (Aeron BackoffIdleStrategy shape: spin->yield->escalating sleep) on all sides both machines; NO CvWait in SPMC v1. (5) shm zombie: slot retirement + epoch|state control word adopted. ADRs recorded: docs/adr/0001 (two machines + no competing-consumer ring), 0002 (lossy soundness: portable fences, symmetric word-atomic copy, T:NoUninit, bench-gated relaxation), 0003 (lifecycle contracts). Design doc docs/design/spmc.md audit-hardened (3 adversarial audits folded). NEXT: PR-1 per §6 phasing (spmc element heap with P-F1/2/3 triple + M-F1/2/5 fixes, loom + panic-injection + benches 1-6).
WAIT-STRATEGY CLARIFICATION (user, 2026-07-05): the v1 family is FOUR self-timed primitive tiers — NoOpWait (tight loop), PauseWait (spin/pause hint), YieldWait, and NEW SleepWait (timed sleep, parkNanos shape) — plus BackoffWait composing them with Aeron-style escalation (spin->yield->sleep). Not 'three + one combo'; Sleep is a standalone tier. Still all notify-free/CrossProcess; still no CvWait in SPMC v1.
DELIVERY SHAPE (user, 2026-07-05): ONE PR containing the whole SPMC implementation, separated by commits (wait strategies -> spmc heap -> broadcast heap -> bytes variants -> shm -> docs), each commit gated (tests/loom/bench/audit) before the next. Branch: spmc-design (already carries design doc + ADRs).
close_reason: Implemented in full on branch spmc-design: spmc + spmc_bytes (gating) and broadcast + broadcast_bytes (lossy), heap + shm (PROT_READ lossy consumers, zombie-retirement gating recovery), SleepWait/BackoffWait/SelfTimed, benches with pre-registered gates, docs. Every commit Miri-gated + adversarially audited; one real data race found & fixed pre-merge (M-F2 bitmap ordering). Perf findings tracked separately (rust-rb-vio, rust-rb-6l0). 250 tests.
